hook: You already know how to find probabilities by counting outcomes or adding up values in a table — but what happens when a variable can take on *any* value in a range, like a person's exact height or the precise time you wait for a bus? Discrete methods break down here, and we need a new framework built on area under a curve rather than sums. By the end of this unit, you'll understand why the bell-shaped curve appears everywhere from test scores to measurement error — and you'll be able to calculate probabilities for it with confidence.
concept: The unifying idea is that for continuous random variables, probability is represented as *area*, and mastering a few key distributions — especially the normal — lets you model and analyze an enormous range of real-world phenomena. Formally, this is captured by the *probability density function* $f(x)$, where $P(a \le X \le b) = \int_a^b f(x)\,dx$.

topic_roadmap.description: You will learn why probability for continuous variables must be measured as area under a curve rather than a sum, and how to interpret a probability density function $f(x)$.
Master the simplest continuous distribution — where every value in an interval is equally likely — and use its rectangular shape to calculate probabilities and find the mean and variance directly.
You will learn to recognize the bell-shaped normal distribution $N(\mu, \sigma^2)$, understand how $\mu$ and $\sigma$ control its center and spread, and use it to model real-world data.

Apply the $z$-score transformation $z = \frac{x - \mu}{\sigma}$ to convert any normal distribution to the standard normal $N(0,1)$, then use $z$-tables to find probabilities and percentiles efficiently.
